You know, the open source question is one that a lot of companies sort of struggle with because at some level, if you're licensing the technology for free, basically, then the value of that technology would be zero, right?
But, you know, some companies basically start, you know, with the patents for, you know, the open source, then they can control whether they want to, you know, charge for it at some point.

And then if they decide to do some sort of, like, let's say open source for one level of product, but then have a premium version that builds on that and adds additional features, then, you know, that the...
The initial patent could be helpful for, you know, those customers that are basically, you know, taking advantage of some of the premium things that you might be doing.
But, you know, it's just one of those things where if you don't do it and you release it, then you basically dedicate it to the public and you can't go back and then try to patent it.

And so, you know, it's kind of a, do you keep your options open and go ahead and file and then, you know, kind of see how things play out and, you know, hopefully keep all of your options open versus, okay, make the decision not to file.

And then, you know, a year or two later after you've released it, realized, oh, darn it, we probably should have, you know, for the pivot or whatever changes are happening in the industry that you're trying to, you know, deal with.
So, you know, there's, there's definitely pros and cons.

framework.

So that's probably the, I guess, biggest data point in terms of what's happened in open source patents and, you know, kind of the litigation side of it.

But yeah, I mean, I think ultimately, even with like an M&A transaction, if all the patent covers is what you're sort of giving people access to for free, you know, the M&A folks would probably say, well, there isn't going to be a lot of value we would give this patent in that context, right?
Because you've basically said it's okay for everybody to use this without having to pay you.

Just to give you some context about how we're thinking about the product and why we're thinking about launching and how, the plan is to have, like, we have an internal way of creating AI workflows, and the entire industry is kind of doing things, stitching, you have a user interface to stitch the workflows together, and we do that in a different way.
We do that in code, and we do that with a language up front that you write the workflow in plain text, and it converts to our version of workflows in code.

This thing is on top already of a bunch of open source things, but we also have certain things in the stack that we came up with ourselves, like the way we store the data, for example, the trace file, the has all the information of what came through, what was processed, and what was generated, that is something that we came up with for ourselves.
So that is a format that we have.
It's nothing crazy, but like...
We have a bunch of things like this in the stack that the combined is different than what everyone else is doing.

So if we were to patent, I don't know how we would patent this because if we would patent the chunks or parts of the whole stack or we would try to patent the whole stack, like what would be the approach there?
You know, and for initial provisional patent filings, trying to basically protect the whole stack is the probably most easy and straightforward way of going about it.
And, you know, we'll basically be able to focus in on the different aspects of what's being covered later.
And some of that will be informed by, you know, doing the conversion to the non-provisional application and getting a search done, seeing what the prior art looks like that comes back.
And, and so.

You know, at this stage, we always recommend casting the net wide because, you know, a lot of times patentability isn't about one particular thing, but it's about the combination of things in a unique way that no one's done before.

So even if individual components, you know, might be open source that you guys are building off of that, but nobody's actually put the glue together the way that you have to be able to enable that, you know, plain text workflow writing.

And then, you know, the trace file, too, that you mentioned, you know, that, I guess trace files have been known and done before, but, you know, the details of what you're doing in the trace file might be, you know, something that ultimately, in combination with everything else, is found to be patentable.
So, you know, one of the things that I was thinking about is, if somebody else were, let's say, you know, to develop a system that's similar to yours, it would seem like you'd have...
Of the ability to detect infringement pretty quickly, right?
Because, you know, a lot of it is sort of this enablement of being able to write a workflow in plain text.
And if somebody else is doing that, then, yeah, chances are good they're doing the underlying things that you've developed to be able to enable that.
So that's good.
You know, sometimes we have people who want to patent things that nobody would ever be able to detect if it was ever infringed.

And that kind of raises, you know, this other aspect of it, which is, you know, if you theoretically don't file, somebody sees what you're doing, and then they decide to build their own version of it, and then they file a patent on it, they theoretically could say, okay, you're infringing their patent, and you've got to stop, you know, offering what you're doing.
So there's a little bit of a risk there, and that's probably going to bring its initial, you know, thought, which was...
It's the defensibility from the perspective of, you know, how do you make sure somebody else doesn't stop you from being able to do what you want to do?
And this is one way to, you know, it's not a silver bullet way necessarily, but it is helpful.

And it's unfortunately one of these things where you never know because it's like, you know, you need like a parallel universe kind of going on where there's one where you filed and one where you didn't.
And then one that you didn't, you somehow were able to see, you know, did somebody else end up filing and then cause you trouble?
Whereas, you know, in this world, you did file and that prevented them from being able to, you know, file on their own and cause you trouble.
So there is that risk ultimately.
But, you know, if you're out there and they want to try to file a year after you've been out there, then you would be prior to them.
So they shouldn't be able to get their patent just by your product being available for, you know, a year.

So, yeah, I mean, it's a bit of a two-edged sword.
People love the fact that it's confidential, so nobody's going to be able to try to go and find it and use it as a blueprint.
But on the other hand, you know, if you don't convert it, then it's not going to be helpful for creating prior art.
That makes it harder for others to get a patent on what you've done.

That's the absolute novelty bar rule.

So yeah, you have, and I'd say U.S.

is probably the important thing to focus on and not necessarily the international, because if the goal is to create prior art to prevent others from being able to copy and file a patent that covers what you guys are doing, then, you know, prior art can exist anywhere in the world.
It isn't that you need to have prior art in every country that you want to prevent somebody from, you know, filing a patent.
So, you know, focusing on the U.S.
is a very reasonable, you know, first filing approach.
